g.data-narration: Welcome! Today we'll explore two fundamental problems that make blockchain work: the CAP theorem and the Byzantine Generals problem.
A distributed system is multiple computers working together as one. Key challenges include concurrency, lack of global clock, and independent failures.
Blockchain must solve two core problems: CAP theorem for data consistency trade-offs, and Byzantine Generals for handling malicious nodes.
g.text: Why Do These Problems Matter for Blockchain?
Trade-off between Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance
Consensus with faulty or malicious nodes

g.data-narration: The CAP theorem states a distributed database can only provide two of three guarantees: Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance.
g.text: A distributed system can provide ONLY
of these three properties during a network partition:

g.data-narration: Consistency means all nodes see the same data at the same time. When written to one node, it must replicate to ALL nodes before success.
g.text: Every read receives the most recent write or an error

g.data-narration: Availability guarantees every request gets a response, but it may not have the latest data. The system stays operational even with failures.
g.text: Every request receives a response, without guarantee of latest data

g.data-narration: Partition Tolerance means the system continues operating despite network failures. Messages may be lost between nodes, but the system survives.
g.text: System continues to operate despite network partitions (message loss between nodes)

g.data-narration: During a network partition, you MUST choose between consistency and availability. You cannot have both - this is the fundamental trade-off.
There are three system types: CA (sacrifices partition tolerance), CP (sacrifices availability), and AP (sacrifices consistency).
g.text: Note: Partitions are inevitable in real distributed systems!

g.data-narration: Blockchains are typically CP systems. They sacrifice availability during consensus to ensure all nodes agree on the ledger state.
g.text: Blockchains prioritize Consistency over Availability during consensus

g.data-narration: The Byzantine Generals Problem: loyal generals must agree on a plan despite traitor generals sending conflicting messages.
A Byzantine fault is the worst-case failure - a node can behave arbitrarily wrong: crash, send wrong data, or act maliciously.
g.text: Mathematical Requirement: Need 3f + 1 nodes to tolerate f Byzantine faults

g.data-narration: Solutions include Oral Messages algorithm (requires 3f+1 nodes), Signed Messages (identifies traitors), and Practical BFT.
PBFT works in rounds: client sends request, primary orders it, all replicas vote prepare and commit, then respond.
